title: Maintain the Median of a Data Stream
description:
### Objective
Design a structure that supports adding integers and querying the median at any time. Maintain two heaps: a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half, rebalancing so their sizes differ by at most one. This is a canonical two-heap pattern.

### Function Contract
Implement `__init__(self: any)` in Python.
Do not change input/output semantics expected by the tests.

### Input and Output
- Input `ops`: type `array` (example: `["addNum","addNum","findMedian","addNum","findMedian"]`).
- Input `args`: type `array` (example: `[[1],[2],[],[3],[]]`).
- Return type: `array`.

### Requirements
- Return the exact expected output for every test case.
- Handle edge cases (empty input, duplicates, boundary values) without raising exceptions.
- Target complexity: time `O(log n) per op`, space `O(n)`.

### Constraints
- Up to 2·10^5 operations
- Values fit 32-bit signed
explanation:
Intuition
Lower half's max and upper half's min pin the middle. Balancing keeps the median at the roots and enables O(1) read after O(log n) updates.

Approach
1. Initial Setup: Push to max-heap (store negative) or to min-heap based on value
2. Main Algorithm: Move one element between heaps if sizes differ by 2
3. Return Result: Median is either the average of roots or the root of the larger heap

Complexity Analysis
- Time Complexity: O(log n) per add
- Space Complexity: O(n)

Key Insights
- Two heaps partition the stream on the fly
- Negating values simulates a max-heap in Python

Common Pitfalls
- Forgetting to rebalance leads to wrong median
- Mixing integer and float division incorrectly
hints: Use two heaps for lower and upper halves
Negate numbers in the lower heap to simulate max-heap
Rebalance when sizes differ by more than one
